title: Expect 100-continue, Early Responses, Body Rejection, and Draining · Go Semantics Case: Early Reject Drain Close
summary: A server reads a huge body before authentication, sends 100 after a final response, drains attacker bytes without bounds, and lets client and server wait on each other forever. Planned artifact: Add and verify the early reject drain close increment in expectation and early-response coordination state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Choose bounded drain versus connection close after early rejection based on remaining framing, risk, reuse value, and deadline.
Bound client waiting for 100, handle early final responses, and avoid sending content after rejection.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Early Reject Drain Close · Theory Early Reject Drain Close
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 4379 in a signed webhook receiver behind an HTTP/2 to HTTP/1.1 gateway. Build deterministic Go evidence to coordinate 100-continue and bounded early rejection without deadlock or unsafe reuse; preserve this operating constraint: one connection has exactly one read owner and one terminal close owner; then minimize a fuzz failure and add it to the deterministic regression corpus. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Choose bounded drain versus connection close after early rejection based on remaining framing, risk, reuse value, and deadline.
